title: Turmeric / Curry on Cotton
stainChemistry: Tannins are polyphenolic compounds that bond to fibers via hydrogen bonding to hydroxyl groups. Curcumin (turmeric's active pigment) is a diketo-enol compound with a highly conjugated chromophore that is particularly resistant to oxidative bleaching. On cotton, tannins oxidize over time, darkening the stain and making it harder to remove. Oxidizing agents (H₂O₂) attack the chromophore, rendering it colorless. Acidic rinse (acetic acid) prevents re-oxidation. UV light is the most effective decolorizer for curcumin (photodegrades the chromophore).
whyThisWorks: Tannins are polyphenolic pigments that bond via hydrogen bonding. H₂O₂ oxidizes the chromophore (colored part) of the molecule into colorless compounds. The oil carrier in curry must be removed first with dry-side solvent, or the color treatment cannot penetrate.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Spray or sponge VDS through the stain. Blot from back side into white absorbent. Ventilate. This removes any waxy or oily component of the curry.
2. Apply Tannin. For old curry/turmeric stains, allow up to 15 min dwell. Watch color fade.
3. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:1 dilute) to stop the oxidation and prevent color rebound.
4. Flush with cool water until cloth shows no residue color. Gentle pressure only.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold/cool — hot water sets tannin stains permanently
warnings: Using hot water — permanently sets the tannin color
Using vinegar on marble or limestone — acid etches the stone permanently
Rubbing the stain instead of blotting — friction drives the stain deeper and damages fibers
Not testing chemicals on a hidden area first — dyes can bleed or bleach unexpectedly

steps: 1. Blot up excess. Apply baking soda paste (baking soda + a little water). Wait 5 minutes.
2. Flush the area with cool water. Blot dry.
3. Apply H₂O₂ 3% + NSD solution. Wait 3–5 minutes.
4. Blot. If stain remains, repeat. Rinse thoroughly. Blot dry.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain persists after these steps, move to professional treatment. Curcumin is highly resistant and requires professional chemistry to avoid damaging the fabric.
warnings: Always test any solution on a hidden area first.
Work from the outside of the stain inward to avoid spreading it.
Use cool or warm water, never hot — heat sets many stains.
Blot, don't rub — friction damages fibers.

neverDo: Use hot water — sets tannin stains permanently
Use ammonia or alkaline cleaners — can darken tannin stains
Rub instead of blot — drives stain deeper and damages fibers
fiberSensitivities: Test all treatments on a hidden area first. Cotton may have specific vulnerabilities depending on dye or finish. For colored cotton, test H₂O₂ on a hidden seam before use.

escalation.when: Stain does not fully resolve after spotting protocol — some set-in curcumin stains require professional bleaching
escalation.whatToTell: Turmeric/curry stain on cotton, curcumin pigment — very resistant to oxidative treatment. Treated at home with H₂O₂ and NSD. Please use professional tannin chemistry. Sunlight exposure after treatment may also help.

contextQuestions: Is this fresh or has it been sitting and drying?
Was this a wet curry sauce or a dry turmeric spice?
Has the garment been through the dryer?
Is the cotton white or colored?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Wet curry sauce has an oil carrier that must be removed first. Dry turmeric bonds very quickly. Dryer exposure before treatment makes removal very difficult. For white cotton: after treatment, brief sunlight exposure on the wet stain can help photodegrade residual curcumin. On colored cotton, test H₂O₂ on a hidden seam first.
